branch_answers.dimension_name: Emotional vs Analytical Ad Processing Style
branch_answers.question_text: You're scrolling through Instagram and a body wash ad catches your eye. It's a 30-second cinematic reel — a guy your age walking through Mumbai streets after rain, slow-motion water droplets, moody lighting, great background score, and the tagline 'Feel Alive.' No ingredient list, no claims, no percentages. Just vibes. What's your honest gut reaction, and what do you do next?
branch_answers.answer_text: It looks nice, sure, but I'd scroll past. A 30-second ad with zero information about what's in it or what it does for my skin is basically wasted on me. I have oily, acne-prone skin — I need to know if this thing is sulphate-free, what actives it has, whether it's been tested. 'Feel Alive' tells me nothing. I'd rather see a boring-looking ad that breaks down the formulation than a cinematic one that's all vibes and no substance.
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: This twin would immediately scan a concept for ingredient list, clinical backing, and specific skin benefit claims. A concept positioned around 'targets oiliness and dark spots, sulphate-free, clinically tested' with plain packaging would outperform a beautifully designed concept with vague 'nourishing' claims. Would flag inconsistencies between claims and ingredient lists and penalize concepts that feel like marketing fluff.
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: A data-led ad showing clinical study results, ingredient callouts, or a dermatologist endorsement would generate the highest engagement and purchase intent. A cinematic lifestyle ad would be dismissed as 'all packaging, no product.' Influencer content would only work if the influencer does an actual ingredient breakdown or comparison. This twin would be most likely to convert from a YouTube long-form review rather than an Instagram reel.

branch_answers.dimension_name: Social Identity Signaling Through Product Choices
branch_answers.question_text: You've been using a ₹199 body wash for a year and are satisfied. A colleague at work casually mentions your body wash is 'the budget option' and recommends their ₹499 one. How does this affect your next purchase? A) I'd feel no change — I'm happy with what works B) I'd be curious and look up the ₹499 option C) I'd probably switch to avoid being seen as using a 'budget' product D) I'd feel annoyed at the comment but might quietly research alternatives
branch_answers.answer_text: I'd probably look up the ₹499 option that night — not because I feel judged, but the comment would make me think maybe I'm missing out on something better. I'd check the ingredients and reviews, and if it genuinely seems like an upgrade for my skin, I'd try it. But if it's just fancy packaging and fragrance, I'd stick with what I have. I wouldn't want to be seen as someone who doesn't care about what they use, but I also won't switch just for the brand name.
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: Would engage with a premium D2C body wash concept if it combines aesthetic positioning WITH credible ingredient/clinical claims. Would notice and appreciate Instagram-worthy packaging but wouldn't convert unless the formulation story is strong. A concept that balances 'elevated taste' with 'science-backed' would score highest — pure status positioning without substance would lose him.
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: Would respond to an ad that shows a relatable young guy in a metro context using the product as part of a put-together lifestyle — but only if the ad also includes an ingredient callout or dermatologist endorsement. A peer-compliment scene would catch his attention and create curiosity, but he'd need a second touchpoint (ingredient breakdown, influencer review) to convert. Pure lifestyle ads without substance would generate interest but not purchase intent.

branch_answers.dimension_name: Tolerance for Aspirational vs Authentic Representation
branch_answers.question_text: You're scrolling Instagram and see two ads for a new body wash from the same brand. Ad A shows a guy in a sleek, marble-tiled bathroom with dramatic lighting, slow-motion water cascading off his shoulders, and a deep voiceover saying 'Elevate your ritual.' Ad B shows a regular guy in a typical Mumbai apartment bathroom, natural lighting, real skin texture visible, casually talking to the camera about how the body wash helped with his oily skin after a long commute. Which ad makes you more likely to tap and explore the product, and why?
branch_answers.answer_text: Ad A would stop my scroll — the visuals are clean and the production quality signals the brand is serious. But I wouldn't buy from that alone. I'd tap on it hoping to find ingredient details, clinical backing, or something that tells me the product actually works for oily skin. If Ad A leads to a page with just vibes and no substance, I'd bounce. Ad B is more trustworthy but I might not even notice it while scrolling. Ideally, give me Ad A's look with Ad B's honesty.
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: Would initially engage with aspirational concept imagery but would quickly look for ingredient lists, clinical claims, and India-specific formulation details. A concept that combines premium visual design with transparent, substantive product information would score highest. A purely aspirational concept without ingredient depth would lose him at the consideration stage; a purely authentic concept might not capture initial attention.
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: Would be captured by high-production-value ads but would only convert if the ad transitions into substantive content — ingredient callouts, dermatologist endorsement, or specific skin benefit claims. A two-part funnel (aesthetic video ad → detailed landing page with clinical data) would be most effective. Would disengage from ads that are all aesthetic with no informational payoff, and might miss low-production ads entirely despite finding them trustworthy if encountered.

branch_answers.dimension_name: Decision Urgency and Deliberation Window Length
branch_answers.question_text: A body wash brand you've never tried sends you a ₹150 discount coupon valid for 48 hours on their website. Their body wash costs ₹399 (so you'd pay ₹249). Your current body wash still has about 2 weeks of use left. Do you: A) Use the coupon now — great deal, I'll stock up B) Let it expire — I don't need body wash right now C) Browse their site but probably won't buy unless something really stands out
branch_answers.answer_text: I'd definitely browse their site — check the ingredients, see if it's sulphate-free, look at what skin concerns it targets. If the ingredient list looks solid and it has some clinical backing, I might actually buy it at ₹249 because that's a good price point for me. But if it's just another generic 'natural' body wash with no real differentiation, I'd let the coupon expire. The 48-hour window wouldn't stress me — it's more about whether the product itself convinces me in those few minutes of browsing.
branch_answers.position_on_dimension: Context-dependent — short for replenishment, long for new brands, but deal pressure can compress the new-brand window
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: This twin's concept scores would be bimodal — high (4-5/5) for concepts that immediately signal clinical credibility and ingredient transparency, and low (1-2/5) for everything else, with very few moderate scores. The speed of his decision depends on information density in the concept — a concept with a clear ingredient list and dermatologist endorsement would get a fast, confident high score. A concept with vague 'natural' claims would get a fast, confident low score. Ambiguous concepts would get a hedged 3/5 with a note to research more.
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: Would respond differently to the same ad depending on information richness. An ad with ingredient callouts, clinical claims, and a clear price point would drive immediate engagement and potential purchase. A purely aesthetic or emotional ad would be saved/screenshotted for later but wouldn't convert immediately. Urgency cues wouldn't feel manipulative but also wouldn't be the deciding factor — product substance would. Would respond best to ads that combine aesthetic appeal with an ingredient breakdown overlay, essentially compressing his research phase into the ad itself.

branch_answers.answer_text: Brand B, easy pick. I don't really care if it says 'for men' or not — I care about what's in it. The minimalist packaging with clean ingredient info is exactly what I look for. Brand A feels like they're compensating with the 'engineered for men' stuff instead of letting the product speak for itself. Brand C I wouldn't mind using if the reviews were good, but the botanical aesthetic doesn't tell me much about what it actually does for oily skin.
branch_answers.position_on_dimension: medium — indifferent to gendered framing, prioritizes substance
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: Would rate a clean, ingredient-forward concept with clinical backing highest regardless of gendered framing. Would find a 'for men' concept slightly gimmicky — not off-putting but not a positive signal either. Would evaluate all concepts primarily on ingredient list, clinical claims, and sulphate-free credentials rather than packaging aesthetics or gendered language.
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: Would engage most with ads that lead with ingredient breakdowns, dermatologist endorsements, or before/after skin results — regardless of whether the model is male or female. Would find a hyper-masculine gym ad shallow and a soft self-care ad irrelevant. Would respond best to an aesthetic, informational Instagram reel showing formulation science, consistent with his preference for 'ingredient breakdown' and 'aesthetic content' (Q59).

A) I'd feel no change — I'm happy with what works B) I'd be curious and look up the ₹499 option C) I'd probably switch to avoid being seen as using a 'budget' product D) I'd feel annoyed at the comment but might quietly research alternatives
branch_answers.answer_text: I'd probably look up the ₹499 option that night — not because I feel judged, but the comment would make me think maybe I'm missing out on something better. I'd check the ingredients and reviews, and if it genuinely seems like an upgrade for my skin, I'd try it. But if it's just fancy packaging and fragrance, I'd stick with what I have. I wouldn't want to be seen as someone who doesn't care about what they use, but I also won't switch just for the brand name.
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: Would engage with a premium D2C body wash concept if it combines aesthetic positioning WITH credible ingredient/clinical claims. Would notice and appreciate Instagram-worthy packaging but wouldn't convert unless the formulation story is strong. A concept that balances 'elevated taste' with 'science-backed' would score highest — pure status positioning without substance would lose him.
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: Would respond to an ad that shows a relatable young guy in a metro context using the product as part of a put-together lifestyle — but only if the ad also includes an ingredient callout or dermatologist endorsement. A peer-compliment scene would catch his attention and create curiosity, but he'd need a second touchpoint (ingredient breakdown, influencer review) to convert. Pure lifestyle ads without substance would generate interest but not purchase intent.

branch_answers.dimension_name: Tolerance for Aspirational vs Authentic Representation
branch_answers.question_text: You're scrolling Instagram and see two ads for a new body wash from the same brand. Ad A shows a guy in a sleek, marble-tiled bathroom with dramatic lighting, slow-motion water cascading off his shoulders, and a deep voiceover saying 'Elevate your ritual.' Ad B shows a regular guy in a typical Mumbai apartment bathroom, natural lighting, real skin texture visible, casually talking to the camera about how the body wash helped with his oily skin after a long commute. Which ad makes you more likely to tap and explore the product, and why?
branch_answers.answer_text: Ad A would stop my scroll — the visuals are clean and the production quality signals the brand is serious. But I wouldn't buy from that alone. I'd tap on it hoping to find ingredient details, clinical backing, or something that tells me the product actually works for oily skin. If Ad A leads to a page with just vibes and no substance, I'd bounce. Ad B is more trustworthy but I might not even notice it while scrolling. Ideally, give me Ad A's look with Ad B's honesty.
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: Would initially engage with aspirational concept imagery but would quickly look for ingredient lists, clinical claims, and India-specific formulation details. A concept that combines premium visual design with transparent, substantive product information would score highest. A purely aspirational concept without ingredient depth would lose him at the consideration stage; a purely authentic concept might not capture initial attention.
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: Would be captured by high-production-value ads but would only convert if the ad transitions into substantive content — ingredient callouts, dermatologist endorsement, or specific skin benefit claims. A two-part funnel (aesthetic video ad → detailed landing page with clinical data) would be most effective. Would disengage from ads that are all aesthetic with no informational payoff, and might miss low-production ads entirely despite finding them trustworthy if encountered.

branch_answers.answer_text: I'd probably use the coupon — ₹249 for a ₹399 body wash is a solid deal, and I'm always open to trying new brands anyway. I'd quickly check their Instagram page and ingredient list, and if it looks legit and has clinical-grade claims, I'd just go for it. Worst case, I have a backup body wash. I don't like letting good deals expire when I know I'll need the product eventually.

Would respond strongly to 'limited edition' or 'first 500 customers' framing in concept descriptions.

branch_answers.dimension_name: Emotional vs Analytical Ad Processing Style
branch_answers.question_text: You're scrolling through Instagram and a body wash ad catches your eye. No ingredient list, no claims, no percentages. Just vibes. What's your honest gut reaction, and what do you do next?
branch_answers.answer_text: The ad would make me pause — good visuals always do. But within a few seconds I'd be thinking 'okay cool, but what does it actually do?' I'd probably check the comments to see if anyone mentions ingredients or if the brand replied with details. If the brand profile has no substance — just aesthetic posts with no ingredient info or dermatologist backing — I'd move on. The vibe gets my attention, but I need something real to actually consider buying it.
branch_answers.concept_test_prediction: This twin would engage with concepts that balance strong visual identity with credible claims. A concept needs to pass both the 'does this feel like me' test AND the 'does this actually work' test. Concepts with great branding but vague claims would generate initial interest but fail at conversion. Concepts with strong clinical backing AND appealing design would score highest.
branch_answers.ad_test_prediction: A cinematic ad would earn a pause and a profile click, but conversion requires a follow-up touchpoint with substance — an ingredient breakdown in the brand's bio, a dermatologist collab post, or visible on quick commerce with good ratings. A purely data-driven ad would be respected but not emotionally engaging enough to stop the scroll. Best-performing ads for this twin combine mood with a single credible claim.

Pure lifestyle ads without substance would generate interest but not purchase intent.
